items.myth: Lower RMSE always means a better model for every business goal.
items.reality: RMSE weights large errors heavily. If the business cares about typical accuracy (not tail risk), MAE may be the better decision metric — or a custom asymmetric loss entirely.
items.example: Delivery ETA: MAE = 5 min (typical) vs RMSE = 12 min (few huge misses). Ops may prefer optimizing MAE even if RMSE is slightly higher on another model.

items.myth: RMSE and MAE require different formulas than MSE and MAE losses.
items.reality: The formulas are the same family — batch MSE and batch MAE. The distinction is role: training loss (Loss Functions) vs evaluation on held-out data (this topic).
items.example: sklearn mean_squared_error on test labels gives MSE; np.sqrt of that is test RMSE — same residuals as training MSE would use on train labels.

items.myth: RMSE can be smaller than MAE.
items.reality: RMSE ≥ MAE always for the same predictions. RMSE equals MAE only when every residual has identical absolute value.
items.example: Residuals [2, −2, 2]: MAE = 2, RMSE = 2. Residuals [0, 0, 6]: MAE = 2, RMSE = √12 ≈ 3.46.

items.myth: You must always report RMSE if you trained with MSE.
items.reality: MSE training suggests RMSE reporting for consistency, but reporting test MAE alongside (or instead) is valid when interpretability of typical error matters more.
items.example: Train with MSELoss; publish test RMSE for benchmark comparison and test MAE for product teams who think in average dollar miss.
